proofs.proof_tex: Proof. Let C_{alpha,beta,s}=P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0), and let R_n(C_{alpha,beta,s},t_0) be the minimax MSE for the causal dose-response value theta_P(t_0)=int_{[0,1]^d} mu_P(t_0,x)p_{X,P}(x) dx. The identifying equality with E_P[Y(t_0)] is the given consistency and ignorability identification statement; the present argument concerns estimation risk for this already identified functional. The quantitative certificate is lem:certified-beta-frontier-bracket. If s>=d/4, that lemma combines the all-beta lower bound with the same-class projection-HOIF upper theorem and lem:rho-oracle-regime-algebra to give constants 0<c<=C<infinity such that c n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} <= R_n(C_{alpha,beta,s},t_0) <= C n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} and rho_n=n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)}. Thus, for every fixed beta>0, the original class has sharp pointwise minimax MSE R_n(C_{alpha,beta,s},t_0) asymp rho_n in the smooth-covariate regime. If 0<s<d/4, lem:certified-beta-frontier-bracket combines thm:frontier-bracket-deficient with lem:rho-deficient-regime-algebra to give c n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} <= R_n(C_{alpha,beta,s},t_0) <= C n^{-2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)}=C rho_n, and the displayed upper exponent is strictly smaller than 2 alpha/(2 alpha+1). Hence the deficient-covariate conclusion is a proved loose bracket, not a matching converse. The terminal unresolved issue is exactly the one stated in def:beta-frontier-handle: within this bracket, decide whether the sharp deficient-regime exponent is the oracle floor, the projection-HOIF functional branch, an intermediate exponent, or a beta-sensitive phase. No proof below asserts a matching lower bound for the functional branch.

added_lemmas.statement: Classical local-polynomial input. Let A be scalar, t be an interior point, K be a compactly supported kernel or equivalent kernel of order at least floor(eta), and h be a bandwidth with h -> 0 and N h -> infinity. If the regression function is eta-Holder in A on a fixed neighborhood of t, the design density is locally bounded above and below at t, and the response has bounded second moment, then the local-polynomial estimator at t has bias O(h^eta) and stochastic L2 size O((N h)^(-1/2)). If J bounded series coefficients in x are estimated inside the same A-window, the stochastic coefficient contribution is O(sqrt(J/(N h))).
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Source proof. This is the standard interior local-polynomial bias and variance theorem for scalar nonparametric regression. See Fan and Gijbels, Local Polynomial Modelling and Its Applications, 1996, Chapter 3, Sections 3.1-3.3, for the equivalent-kernel expansion and the pointwise bias-variance formulas, and Tsybakov, Introduction to Nonparametric Estimation, 2009, Chapter 1, for the Holder-class kernel and local-polynomial rates. Applying those formulas componentwise to J bounded regressors gives variance proportional to J/(N h), hence stochastic L2 size sqrt(J/(N h)).

added_lemmas.statement: Classical series/sieve L2 input. For an s-Holder function on [0,1]^d and a bounded spline, wavelet, or tensor-product polynomial sieve of dimension L with the usual approximation property, there is a sieve element f_L with ||f-f_L||_2 <= C L^{-s/d}. Least-squares or ridge series estimation over this sieve with effective sample size N, bounded second moments, and bounded regressors has prediction L2 error O(L^{-s/d}+sqrt(L/N)). The same rate applies to projection density estimation in L2 for an s-Holder density bounded by a finite envelope.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Source proof. The approximation statement is the standard Holder approximation property of splines, compactly supported wavelets, and tensor-product local polynomial sieves. The prediction-rate statement is Newey, Journal of Econometrics 79, 1997, Sections 2-3; Chen, Handbook of Econometrics, Volume 6B, Chapter 76, 2007, sieve approximation and convergence-rate sections; and Belloni, Chernozhukov, Chetverikov and Kato, Journal of Econometrics 186, 2015, Theorem 4.1 and Section 3 approximation examples. These results yield L2 prediction rates of order approximation error plus sqrt(L/N) under bounded moments and stable regularized series estimation. For densities, the orthogonal-series or wavelet projection estimator has the same bias-variance decomposition in L2.

added_lemmas.statement: Higher-order influence function projection input. For a localized dose-response partial-mean functional with a scalar treatment localization bandwidth h and a J-dimensional covariate projection space, the cross-fitted order-m projection-HOIF estimator has the following non-remainder MSE components: treatment smoothing bias squared O(h^{2 alpha}), first-order localized variance O((n h)^(-1)), covariate projection product bias squared O(J^{-4s/d}), and degenerate projected U-statistic variance O(J/(n h)^2).
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Source proof. This is the higher-order influence-function projection risk calculation of Robins, Li, Tchetgen Tchetgen and van der Vaart, IMS Collections 2, 2008, Sections 2-3, combined with the continuous-treatment localization and pointwise dose-response estimator of Bonvini and Kennedy, arXiv:2207.11825, Theorem 3.1 and the discussion of the pointwise dose-response rate. The source formulas give the first-order localized variance, the projection approximation term for the product of two covariate residuals, and the degenerate U-statistic variance for the empirical projection kernel. Specializing the two covariate smoothness indices to the common value s converts the projection approximation into J^{-4s/d}.

added_lemmas.statement: Higher-order nuisance-remainder input. For a cross-fitted order-m HOIF estimator, after the smoothing bias, projection bias, and degenerate U-statistic variance have been separated, the remaining estimation bias is a finite sum of products of m+1 nuisance estimation errors in L2 norm. Consequently, if the largest nuisance L2 error is delta_n, the squared contribution of this orthogonal remainder is at most C delta_n^{2(m+1)}.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Source proof. This is the order-m von Mises and U-statistic remainder structure in Robins, Li, Tchetgen Tchetgen and van der Vaart, Higher order influence functions and minimax estimation of nonlinear functionals, IMS Collections 2, 2008, Sections 2-3, DOI 10.1214/193940307000000527, and the companion technical report arXiv:1601.05820, where the full product-remainder proofs omitted from the IMS version are supplied. Cross-fitting makes the nuisance estimates independent of the evaluation fold, so repeated Cauchy-Schwarz bounds turn each product term into a product of L2 nuisance errors.

added_lemmas.statement: Deterministic denominator control. If f and fhat are functions on a probability space, c <= f <= C, and fhat is truncated to [c/2,2C], then ||1/fhat-1/f||_2 <= 2 c^{-2} ||fhat-f||_2 and ||fhat-f||_2 is not increased by truncation up to a universal constant. The same statement applies to conditional treatment densities and their reciprocals on the local positivity neighborhood.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Proof. On the interval [c/2,2C], the map z -> 1/z has derivative bounded in absolute value by 4/c^2. Hence |1/fhat-1/f| <= 4 c^{-2}|fhat-f| pointwise, and the displayed L2 inequality follows after changing the numerical constant. Truncation to a closed interval is the metric projection onto that interval on the real line, so it is one-Lipschitz pointwise. If f already lies in the interval, then |T(fhat)-f| <= |fhat-f| for the truncation map T. Local positivity supplies the lower bound for pi_P near t_0; boundedness and the chosen truncation supply the upper interval.

added_lemmas.statement: Fix beta>0. Let K_h be a compactly supported local-polynomial equivalent kernel of order at least floor(max(alpha,beta)) at the interior dose t_0. On each cross-fitting training fold, estimate mu_P(t_0,.), pi_P(t_0|.), g_P(t_0,.)=1/pi_P(t_0|.), and p_{X,P} by bounded and positivity-truncated local-polynomial-in-A plus L-dimensional covariate-sieve estimators, with p_X estimated globally. Then, uniformly over P in P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0), the fold-specific estimators satisfy root-L2 bounds ||muhat-mu||_{K_h,2} <= C(h^alpha+L^{-s/d}+sqrt(L/(n h))), ||pihat-pi||_{K_h,2}+||ghat-g||_{K_h,2} <= C(h^beta+L^{-s/d}+sqrt(L/(n h))), and ||phat_X-p_X||_2 <= C(L^{-s/d}+sqrt(L/n)), in probability and in mean square after truncation.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Proof. Work on one training fold; the fold size is a fixed fraction of n, so replacing n by the fold size only changes constants. The interior-dose assumption gives a fixed neighborhood on which K_h is supported for all sufficiently small h. For mu, the local-polynomial source lemma gives treatment-coordinate bias O(h^alpha) because a -> mu_P(a,x) is alpha-Holder. The series source lemma gives a covariate approximation error O(L^{-s/d}) for x -> mu_P(t_0,x), and the localized effective sample size is n h, so the J-coefficient stochastic term with dimension L is O(sqrt(L/(n h))). This proves the displayed mu rate. For pi, the same argument applies to the conditional treatment density or equivalently the localized joint density divided by p_X: beta-Holder smoothness in A gives O(h^beta), s-Holder smoothness in x gives O(L^{-s/d}), and the local window gives stochastic size O(sqrt(L/(n h))). The reciprocal nuisance g=1/pi inherits the same rate by lem:truncation-reciprocal-lipschitz and local positivity. Finally, p_X is estimated without treatment localization, so the effective sample size is n and the series density result gives O(L^{-s/d}+sqrt(L/n)). Bounded truncation preserves the rates and supplies the deterministic positivity and envelope controls needed downstream. The beta-dependence is isolated in h^beta: for every fixed beta>0 this is a positive power of n under the bandwidths used below.

added_lemmas.statement: For fixed beta>0, bandwidth h, projection dimension J, pilot nuisance dimension L, and HOIF order m, define the data-driven cross-fitted localized projection-HOIF estimator hat theta_{h,J,L,m} as follows: estimate the nuisances on training folds as in lem:bounded-sieve-local-polynomial-rates; on held-out folds evaluate the first-order localized influence function for theta_h(t_0); and add the order-2 through order-m degenerate U-statistic corrections obtained by projecting the covariate residual factors onto a J-dimensional bounded basis. Then, uniformly over P in P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0), E_P[(hat theta_{h,J,L,m}-theta_P(t_0))^2] <= C{h^{2 alpha}+(n h)^(-1)+J^{-4s/d}+J/(n h)^2}+Rem_{n,m,L,h}.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Proof. The estimator is an explicit sample map because the folds, the local-polynomial nuisance fits, the truncations, the J-dimensional projection matrix, and the finite-order U-statistic kernels are all computed from the observed sample. Let theta_h(P) denote the local-polynomial smoothed target obtained by replacing evaluation at t_0 with the equivalent kernel K_h in the treatment coordinate. Since K_h has order at least floor(alpha) and a -> mu_P(a,x) is alpha-Holder uniformly in x, the standard kernel moment cancellation gives |theta_h(P)-theta_P(t_0)| <= C h^alpha, hence squared smoothing bias O(h^{2 alpha}). The first-order localized influence function contains one factor K_h(A-t_0)/pi_P(t_0|X) times a bounded outcome residual plus a bounded regression term. Local positivity and bounded outcomes give variance O(h^{-1}), so averaging n observations gives O((n h)^(-1)). The HOIF projection step approximates the product of two s-Holder covariate-side residuals by the J-dimensional projection space. Each residual has L2 approximation error O(J^{-s/d}); the second-order product bias is O(J^{-2s/d}) in absolute value, so its squared contribution is O(J^{-4s/d}). The order-2 and higher projected kernels are degenerate after cross-fitting and centering. The projection rank is J and the treatment localization contributes h^{-1} on each of two kernel factors, yielding variance O(J/(n h)^2) for the leading degenerate U-statistic term; higher fixed-order degenerate terms are bounded by the same order or smaller under the bounded-kernel envelope. These four terms are exactly the non-remainder terms in lem:hoif-projection-risk-source. All remaining effects of replacing true nuisances by cross-fit estimated and truncated nuisances are collected in Rem_{n,m,L,h}; their product form is handled separately in lem:hoif-order-remainder-negligible.

added_lemmas.statement: Let delta_mu,n, delta_pi,n, and delta_x,n be the cross-fit L2 nuisance rates from lem:bounded-sieve-local-polynomial-rates for bandwidth h_n=n^{-eta_h+o(1)} and pilot dimension L_n=n^{eta_L+o(1)}, with 0<eta_h<1 and 0<eta_L<1-eta_h. Let delta_n=max(delta_mu,n,delta_pi,n,delta_x,n). Then delta_n=O(n^{-lambda_*+o(1)}) with lambda_*=min{alpha eta_h, beta eta_h, s eta_L/d, (1-eta_h-eta_L)/2, (1-eta_L)/2}>0. For the order-m_n projection-HOIF estimator, after the smoothing, projection, and U-statistic terms are separated, Rem_{n,m_n,L_n,h_n} <= C delta_n^{2(m_n+1)}. If the target MSE rate is rho_n=n^{-kappa+o(1)} and m_n >= ceil(kappa/(2 lambda_*)), then Rem_{n,m_n,L_n,h_n}=o(rho_n).
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Proof. Substitute h_n=n^{-eta_h+o(1)} and L_n=n^{eta_L+o(1)} into lem:bounded-sieve-local-polynomial-rates. The outcome-regression treatment bias contributes n^{-alpha eta_h+o(1)}. The treatment-density and reciprocal treatment-density bias contributes n^{-beta eta_h+o(1)}. The covariate approximation term contributes n^{-s eta_L/d+o(1)}. The localized stochastic pilot term contributes n^{-(1-eta_h-eta_L)/2+o(1)}, and the global p_X stochastic term contributes n^{-(1-eta_L)/2+o(1)}. Taking the maximum over the nuisance rates gives delta_n=O(n^{-lambda_*+o(1)}). The inequalities 0<eta_h<1, 0<eta_L<1-eta_h, and beta,alpha,s>0 imply lambda_*>0. By lem:hoif-remainder-product-source, the squared contribution of the order-m_n orthogonal nuisance remainder is bounded by C delta_n^{2(m_n+1)}=O(n^{-2 lambda_*(m_n+1)+o(1)}). If m_n >= ceil(kappa/(2 lambda_*)), then m_n+1>kappa/(2 lambda_*), so 2 lambda_*(m_n+1)>kappa. Therefore n^{-2 lambda_*(m_n+1)+o(1)}=o(n^{-kappa+o(1)})=o(rho_n).

added_lemmas.statement: For every fixed beta>0 and every s>=d/4, the cross-fitted localized projection-HOIF estimator on P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0), with h_n asymp n^{-1/(2 alpha+1)}, projection dimension J_n asymp n h_n, pilot nuisance dimension L_n asymp n^{alpha/(2 alpha+1)}, and HOIF order m_n chosen as in lem:hoif-order-remainder-negligible, satisfies sup_{P in P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0)} E_P[(hat theta_n-theta_P(t_0))^2] <= C n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} for all sufficiently large n.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Proof. Fix beta>0 and s>=d/4. Set h_n=n^{-eta_h} with eta_h=1/(2 alpha+1), set the HOIF projection dimension J_n proportional to n h_n=n^{2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)}, and set the nuisance pilot dimension L_n proportional to n^{alpha/(2 alpha+1)}. The distinction between J_n and L_n is only a tuning distinction: J_n controls the projection bias and degenerate U-statistic variance, while L_n is the dimension of the training-fold nuisance estimators. From lem:localized-projection-hoif-risk-decomposition, the non-remainder MSE is bounded by C{h_n^{2 alpha}+(n h_n)^(-1)+J_n^{-4s/d}+J_n/(n h_n)^2}+Rem_n. The first two terms satisfy h_n^{2 alpha}=n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} and (n h_n)^(-1)=n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)}. Since J_n asymp n h_n, the degenerate U-statistic variance term is J_n/(n h_n)^2 asymp (n h_n)^(-1)=n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)}. The projection term is J_n^{-4s/d}=n^{-(2 alpha/(2 alpha+1))(4s/d)}, which is at most n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} exactly when 4s/d>=1, i.e. s>=d/4. It remains to control Rem_n. Here eta_L=alpha/(2 alpha+1), so 0<eta_L<1-eta_h=2 alpha/(2 alpha+1). Lemma lem:hoif-order-remainder-negligible gives lambda_*=min{alpha/(2 alpha+1), beta/(2 alpha+1), s alpha/(d(2 alpha+1)), alpha/(2(2 alpha+1)), (alpha+1)/(2 alpha+1)/2}>0. With kappa=2 alpha/(2 alpha+1), choose m_n >= ceil(kappa/(2 lambda_*)). Then Rem_n=o(n^{-kappa}). Combining the displayed bounds proves the asserted same-class upper risk bound. The constant and the required order may depend on the fixed beta but not on n.

added_lemmas.statement: Assume ass:baseline-submodel-slack. For every fixed beta>0 and every s>=d/4, there are constants 0<c<=C<infinity such that, for all sufficiently large n, c n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} <= R_n(P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0),t_0) <= C n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)}. Hence the smooth-covariate same-class minimax rate is sharp and equals rho_n.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Proof. Fix beta>0 and s>=d/4, and abbreviate C_{alpha,beta,s}=P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0). The lower side is thm:sharp-pointwise-lower-bound: under ass:baseline-submodel-slack there is c_l>0 such that R_n(C_{alpha,beta,s},t_0) >= c_l n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} for all sufficiently large n. The upper side is supplied by the data-driven estimator in lem:same-class-hoif-upper-smooth-covariates: there is C_u<infinity such that sup_{P in C_{alpha,beta,s}} E_P[(hat theta_n-theta_P(t_0))^2] <= C_u n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)}. Since def:minimax-risk takes the infimum over all estimators, this single estimator implies R_n(C_{alpha,beta,s},t_0) <= C_u n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)}. Taking c=c_l and C=C_u proves the two-sided minimax inequality. Finally, lem:rho-oracle-regime-algebra gives rho_n=n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} when s>=d/4, so the sharp rate equals the published rho_n expression in this regime.

added_lemmas.statement: For every fixed beta>0 and every 0<s<d/4, the same cross-fitted localized projection-HOIF estimator on P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0), with the projection dimension re-optimized for the deficient regime, satisfies sup_{P in P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0)} E_P[(hat theta_n-theta_P(t_0))^2] <= C n^{-2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)} for all sufficiently large n.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Proof. Fix beta>0 and 0<s<d/4. Put D_s=4s+alpha(d+4s), eta_h=4s/D_s, eta_J=2 alpha d/D_s, and kappa=8 alpha s/D_s. Equivalently, kappa=2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha). Choose h_n asymp n^{-eta_h} and projection dimension J_n asymp n^{eta_J}; this is the same as J_n asymp (n h_n)^{2d/(d+4s)} because n h_n has exponent 1-eta_h=alpha(d+4s)/D_s. Start from lem:localized-projection-hoif-risk-decomposition. The smoothing term is h_n^{2 alpha}=n^{-2 alpha eta_h}=n^{-8 alpha s/D_s}=n^{-kappa}. The two covariate terms balance: J_n^{-4s/d}=n^{-(2 alpha d/D_s)(4s/d)}=n^{-8 alpha s/D_s}=n^{-kappa}, and J_n/(n h_n)^2=n^{eta_J-2(1-eta_h)}=n^{-8 alpha s/D_s}=n^{-kappa}. The oracle variance term is (n h_n)^(-1)=n^{-alpha(d+4s)/D_s}; because s<d/4 implies d+4s>8s, this term is o(n^{-kappa}). For the nuisance pilots choose L_n=n^{eta_L} with eta_L=(1-eta_h)/2. Then 0<eta_L<1-eta_h, and lem:hoif-order-remainder-negligible gives lambda_*=min{alpha eta_h, beta eta_h, s eta_L/d, (1-eta_h-eta_L)/2, (1-eta_L)/2}>0. Choose m_n >= ceil(kappa/(2 lambda_*)). The remainder is o(n^{-kappa}). Rounding J_n and L_n to integers changes only constants. Therefore the estimator has MSE at most C n^{-kappa}=C n^{-2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)} uniformly over the class.

added_lemmas.statement: For every fixed beta>0 and every 0<s<d/4, under the declared same-class assumptions and ass:baseline-submodel-slack, there exist constants 0<c<=C<infinity such that, for all sufficiently large n, c n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} <= R_n(P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0),t_0) <= C n^{-2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)}.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Proof. Fix beta>0 and 0<s<d/4, and write C_{alpha,beta,s}=P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0). The lower endpoint is exactly lem:oracle-dose-regression-lower-all-beta: under ass:baseline-submodel-slack there is c_l>0 such that R_n(C_{alpha,beta,s},t_0) >= c_l n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} for all sufficiently large n. That lower construction perturbs only mu_P and keeps p_{X,P} and pi_P fixed at the slack baseline, so it is valid for every s and every beta>0. The upper endpoint is supplied by lem:same-class-hoif-upper-deficient-covariates: there is a data-driven estimator with sup_{P in C_{alpha,beta,s}} E_P[(hat theta_n-theta_P(t_0))^2] <= C_u n^{-2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)}. By the infimum over estimators in def:minimax-risk, R_n(C_{alpha,beta,s},t_0) <= C_u n^{-2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)}. Taking c=c_l and C=C_u gives the displayed bracket. This is an honest loose two-sided bracket: no matching deficient-regime converse for the upper exponent is used or asserted.

added_lemmas.statement: Assume ass:baseline-submodel-slack. For every fixed beta>0, if s>=d/4 then R_n(P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0),t_0) asymp n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)}=rho_n. For every fixed beta>0, if 0<s<d/4 then c n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} <= R_n(P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0),t_0) <= C n^{-2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)}=C rho_n for all sufficiently large n, and 2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)<2 alpha/(2 alpha+1).
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Proof. Fix beta>0 and abbreviate C_{alpha,beta,s}=P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0). If s>=d/4, thm:sharp-minimax-smooth-covariate gives constants 0<c_s<=C_s<infinity such that c_s n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} <= R_n(C_{alpha,beta,s},t_0) <= C_s n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} for all sufficiently large n. Lemma lem:rho-oracle-regime-algebra identifies rho_n with n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)}, so this is R_n(C_{alpha,beta,s},t_0) asymp rho_n. If 0<s<d/4, thm:frontier-bracket-deficient gives constants 0<c_d<=C_d<infinity such that c_d n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} <= R_n(C_{alpha,beta,s},t_0) <= C_d n^{-2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)}. Lemma lem:rho-deficient-regime-algebra identifies rho_n=n^{-2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)} and proves 2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)<2 alpha/(2 alpha+1). Substitution yields the displayed deficient-regime bracket with a strict exponent gap. Constants may depend on the fixed smoothness indices, beta, s, d, radii, and baseline slack, but not on n.

open_obligations.what_is_open: For 0<s<d/4 and fixed beta>0, determine the sharp minimax MSE exponent inside the proved bracket c n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} <= R_n(P_{alpha,beta,s}(M,c_0,epsilon_0,t_0),t_0) <= C n^{-2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)}.
open_obligations.obstruction: The all-beta oracle lower bound perturbs only mu_P and therefore proves the treatment-regression floor, while the projection-HOIF upper theorem proves the functional branch. The current proof does not provide a dominated localized fuzzy converse with separation of order n^{-1/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)}, and it also does not provide an estimator proving that the oracle floor is attainable when s<d/4. Thus the matching deficient-regime exponent is not identified by the declared nodes.
open_obligations.attempted: The proof uses the repaired same-class projection-HOIF decomposition, explicit nuisance rates, and order-m remainder control to prove the upper branch, and it uses the existing pure-mu Le Cam lower branch for the lower endpoint. It deliberately does not reuse the earlier deterministic-outcome fuzzy construction and does not convert the missing functional-branch converse into an assumption.
open_obligations.partial_result: The certified partial result is complete: R_n asymp n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)}=rho_n for s>=d/4, and c n^{-2 alpha/(2 alpha+1)} <= R_n <= C n^{-2/(1+d/(4s)+1/alpha)}=C rho_n with strict exponent gap for 0<s<d/4.
